text: While the past few decades saw a pronounced shift toward all-volunteer forces across Europe, the deteriorating regional security landscape has reignited a debate leading several nations to reconsider the necessity of compulsory service. Contemporary conscription systems, however, are not mere relics of the past; they have evolved significantly to align with modern social structures and the rising prominence of individualistic and neoliberal values. This study examines the diverse frameworks of military conscription and evaluates the continued viability of the model in six European nations: Estonia, Finland, Lithuania, Norway, Sweden, and Switzerland. While Estonia, Finland, Norway, and Switzerland have maintained continuous service requirements, Lithuania and Sweden have only recently reinstated them. Despite their structural differences, a common trend emerges across these countries: an increasing focus on individual motivation and the integration of women. By analyzing the proportion of the birth cohort drafted, the drivers of service motivation, and institutional approaches to female participation, this article classifies these six nations into three distinct system types. Ultimately, the findings demonstrate that instead of stagnating, conscription has remained a resilient and adaptive policy tool, evolving in tandem with both shifting security demands and broader societal transformations.
